last_action: all 23 tasks done — 416 Python + 1647 JS tests pass, 9/9 drift proofs held

FR-001.title: Capability registrations are stored in a dedicated registry file outside the folder the routine cleanup step restores

FR-002.title: The registry file is ordinary project configuration committed to version control

FR-003.title: The registry file sits at a top-level, self-describing path that is findable and hand-editable

FR-004.title: The living-specs on/off switch lives in the registry file alongside the capabilities

FR-005.title: Readers prefer the registry file and fall back to the old location when only it has registrations
tests: livingSpecsModel.test.ts::falls back to the legacy config when no registry exists

FR-006.title: When both locations hold registrations, readers use the registry file only and warn about the stale block
tests: livingSpecsModel.test.ts::prefers the registry and flags the stale legacy block when both exist

FR-007.title: Writers write to the registry file and move old-location registrations into it as part of that write

FR-008.title: A migrating writer removes only the capability block from the old file, leaving every other setting intact

FR-009.title: A writer reports in plain language when it has moved registrations to the registry file

FR-010.title: A project with neither location reads as not adopted: nothing reported, success exit, no warning
tests: livingSpecsModel.test.ts::reads as not adopted when neither location exists

FR-011.title: A writer refuses to write, without truncating, when the target file cannot be parsed

FR-012.title: Nested-project boundary detection recognizes either the registry file or the old settings file
tests: livingSpecsModel.test.ts::treats a nested directory with only a registry as its own project

FR-013.title: Living Specs view refresh reacts to edits of the registry file

FR-014.title: All documentation and command instructions name the registry file instead of the old location

FR-015.title: The project workflow documents carrying the cleanup step state that registrations are no longer at risk

context: area: src/features/specs/livingSpecsModel.ts and the Living Specs sidebar
constraint: extension isolation — shipped code must not depend on .claude/**
constraint: two extensions, two changelogs — speckit-extension/ changes never touch the root docs
intent: Give living-specs capability registrations their own committed, top-level file so the routine cleanup step can no longer silently delete them.
expectations: Do not narrow or rewrite the git restore cleanup line itself
Do not move pipeline hook or recipe settings out of the old location
Do not change how capabilities resolve, match, or order — only where they are stored
No version bumps in any manifest
approach: Move the livingSpecs block from .specify/companion.yml to a new project-root living-specs.yml. One shared location rule per runtime — companion_config.py for the scripts, livingSpecsModel.ts for the editor — so no consumer re-derives the path. Readers prefer the registry and fall back to the old file; writers always write the registry and lift the block out of the old file in the same operation, reporting the move.

decisions.why: Outside .specify/ so the routine cleanup cannot sweep it; committed like any root config; named for the feature the docs teach so it is findable on the first guess
decisions.rejected: Another path under .specify/ (still swept); narrowing the cleanup line itself (every workflow doc must change and the old line gets pasted back); capabilities.yml (too generic); a hidden .companion/ dir (a directory for one file)

decisions.decision: Read-time fallback to the old location, migrate on the next write
decisions.why: Reads run from sidebar refreshes and read-only checkouts, so they must have no side effects; a write is a moment the person deliberately chose to change configuration
decisions.rejected: Migrating on first read (mutates two tracked files from a background refresh); fallback with no migration at all (the old location never retires)

decisions.decision: The registry wins outright when both locations have registrations, and a malformed registry does not fall back
decisions.why: Merging two partial sets yields a state matching neither file and makes deletion impossible; falling back on a parse error would silently resurrect a set the person believes they replaced
decisions.rejected: Merge by capability name; fall back to legacy when the registry is unparseable

decisions.decision: Flattened top-level shape in the new file, with the livingSpecs wrapper still accepted
decisions.why: A dedicated file does not need a wrapper key, and accepting one anyway makes the obvious hand-migration (copy the old block across) just work
decisions.rejected: Keeping the wrapper as the only shape to reuse the existing splice helper byte-for-byte

decisions.decision: The enabled switch and the drift exempt list travel with the capabilities
decisions.why: The switch and the registrations are one decision; leaving the switch in the swept file would leave half the bug in place
decisions.rejected: Keeping enabled as a setting in .specify/companion.yml

decisions.decision: Kept the location rule inside the existing companion_config.py rather than a new module
decisions.why: A new runtime script would have to be added to both packing lists (package-manifest.py and .vscodeignore); reusing an already-shipped module keeps both lists untouched

plan.summary: Planned the move of capability registrations to a project-root living-specs.yml with read-time fallback and migrate-on-write.
plan.key_finding: Every Python reader already funnels through resolve-spec-paths.load_living and every editor reader through livingSpecsModel.readLivingSpecs, so the location rule needs exactly two homes and no consumer re-derives it.

tasks.summary: 23 tasks across 7 phases: setup, a shared location rule, one phase per user story, and a polish phase for docs, goldens, verification, and drift-proofing.

implement.summary: Capability registrations moved to a project-root living-specs.yml that no cleanup step can sweep, with read-time fallback and migrate-on-write for existing adopters.

T001.did: Added the registry-shaped fixture the new Python tests read

T002.did: Added the shared location rule to the Python loader: registry-first resolution with legacy fallback, a boundary probe matching either file, and the registry renderer and splice

T003.did: Gave the editor-side reader the same rule: registry-first parse, legacy fallback, inert on an unparseable registry, legacyStale on the listing, boundary probe matching either file

T004.did: Routed the resolver through the shared rule: load_living now resolves the registry, load_living_with_meta exposes origin and warnings, and the boundary probe delegates to the shared one

T005.did: Added the survival test that registers a capability, runs the real git restore .specify/ cleanup, and asserts the registration still resolves

T006.did: Pointed the registration writer at the project-root registry: renders the flattened block, splices into an existing file so its comments survive, and reports living-specs.yml as the config path

T009.did: Added migrate-on-write to the registration writer: a legacy-only or stale block is removed from .specify/companion.yml in the same operation, reported in plain language, and carried as migratedFrom

T007.did: Pointed the relocation writer at the registry, reusing the shared renderer and splice, and taught its rollback to remove a registry it created

T008.did: Added the migration tests: legacy-only reads resolve, a later registration moves the whole set and strips only the old block, both-present prefers the registry and warns stale, and an unparseable registry never falls back

T010.did: Added migrate-on-write to the relocation writer, sharing the registration writer's legacy-block removal

T011.did: Added the hand-edit tests: flattened and wrapped shapes resolve identically, a hand-written comment survives a later registration, and a nested registry marks its own project

T015.did: Added the not-adopted test: with neither file present the resolver, drift, and coverage each report nothing, exit zero, and print no warning

T012.did: Added the editor-side tests: registry-first read, legacy fallback, stale-legacy flag, wrapper shape, inert on unparseable, not-adopted, and nested-project pruning

T013.did: Added living-specs.yml to the Living Specs refresh watcher so edits to the registry refresh the view

T014.did: Updated the Living Specs empty-state tooltip to name the registry file

T016.did: Updated the adoption, relocation, drift, coverage, and mark-complete command bodies to name the registry file

T017.did: Updated the specify node's living-specs gate prose to name the registry, with the legacy block still recognized

T018.did: Rewrote the spec-kit README's living-specs config examples for the new file and shape, and added the user-facing Unreleased changelog entry

T019.did: Updated the root changelog's living-specs references and added the Unreleased entry for the editor half

T020.did: Noted in the four workflow documents carrying the cleanup line that capability registrations now sit outside the restored folder

T021.did: Reassembled the command bodies from the edited nodes and re-blessed the goldens

T022.did: Ran the full verification set: compile, 1647 JS tests, 416 Python tests, node assembly, shape parity, command emissions, and the package manifest — all green

T023.did: Drift-proofed all nine new behaviors: reverted each one, confirmed the guarding test failed, and restored

verified.command: register-capability.py --name provetrap --match src/core/** && git restore .specify/ && resolve-spec-paths.py --changed src/core/constants.ts
verified.result: The capability survives the cleanup and still resolves; before the change it was silently erased
compile clean; 1647 tests in 120 suites pass
all four green after reassembling commands and re-blessing goldens
warnings: assemble-nodes reported a diff before the rebuild, which is the expected signal after editing a node

verified.command: revert each new behavior, run the guarding test, restore
verified.result: 9 of 9 reverts produced the expected failure: registry location, migrate-on-write, malformed-no-fallback (both runtimes), boundary probe (both runtimes), stale-legacy warning, wrapper shape, and registry-first read
